title: Extra Time
trend: World Cup 2026 finale week — riding the shared 'edge of seat' emotion of extra time/penalties, theme only: no real teams, players, scorelines, or footage. Time-sensitive, best posted during/just after finale week.
hook: The whole world just watched 120 minutes of two teams refusing to lose. Your last 'match' has been in extra time for three weeks.
script:
0:00-0:01 — Our own mocked scoreboard graphic (no real team names/crests): '90:00 — STILL 0-0.' Beat of crowd-tension sound (royalty-free, our own).
0:01-0:03 — On-screen text punches in: 'meanwhile, your last dating-app match:' cut to a mocked chat preview, three dots typing... forever.
0:03-0:06 — Scoreboard graphic flips: '90+7. STILL TYPING.' Comedic tension sting (our own audio, not the meme's original clip).
0:06-0:10 — Rapid on-screen text escalation, one line per fast cut: 'extra time.' → 'still extra time.' → 'penalties?' → 'nobody's taking a penalty.' (chaotic-cut pacing, no VO needed)
0:10-0:14 — Hard cut to daylight: two people mid-laugh after a rally on a padel/tennis court. On-screen text: 'meanwhile, this took 45 minutes, start to finish.'
0:14-0:18 — On-screen text only: 'no extra time. no penalties. you just show up and the game decides.'
0:18-0:22 — End card: KeepItUp motion-arc mark on anthracite, on-screen text 'meet through movement — open worldwide, early access,' caption-only line: 'link in bio before this goes to another shootout.'
caption: The world just sat through the tensest 30 minutes of the year waiting for a result. Your group chat's 'we should hang out sometime' has been in stoppage time since spring. We built the version with no extra time — you just show up and the game decides. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment 'PENALTIES' if your last match has been stuck in stoppage time longer than you'd like to admit.
imageConcept: Cover/thumbnail frame: anthracite (#20262B) background styled like a stadium scoreboard, bold neon-green (#3BEA7E) digits '90+7’' large center, off-white caption underneath 'STILL TYPING.' No real team crests, colors, or names — purely our own generic scoreboard graphic. KeepItUp mark small bottom-right corner.

title: The 5 Stages Of 'We Should Hang Out Sometime'
trend: Countdown + absurd-but-true micro-story format — a comedic countdown that reads like a tiny tragedy everyone recognizes.
hook: A true story that never actually happened. Countdown: 5 stages of 'we should hang out sometime.' 👇
slides: A true story that never happened. The 5 stages of 'we should hang out sometime.' 👇
Stage 5: the plan is born. Energy: immaculate. Voice memo length: 47 seconds.
Stage 4: 'this week is crazy, what about next week?' Next week has entered the chat.
Stage 3: the thread goes quiet. Not a fight. Just... gravity.
Stage 2: someone posts a story from somewhere fun. The other person likes it. That's the whole interaction now.
Stage 1: six months later, 'we should really catch up' — the sequel nobody asked for.
Stage 0: there's already a small local game happening near you tonight. No planning. No thread. Just a time and a place.
Open worldwide. Early access — link in bio before this becomes stage 6. 🎾
caption: This is a true story that happens approximately 4 million times a week and has never once ended in stage 0. We got tired of watching good intentions die in a group chat, so we skipped straight to 'there's a game tonight, just come.' Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment which stage your longest-running 'we should hang out' thread has been stuck on.
imageConcept: 8-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background throughout. Cards 2-6 styled as a descending 'stage counter' — a bold neon-green number (5→0) top-left of each card like a countdown timer, off-white body text. Card 7 shifts tone: neon-green highlight background, off-white bold text, small running-figure/racket icon. Card 8 (reveal): KeepItUp wordmark centered, neon-green 'link in bio' arrow, off-white 'early access, open worldwide.'

title: Sartre Never Played Mixed Doubles
trend: Ironic, self-aware philosophical-quote-with-a-twist format — dry humor, not a motivational poster.
hook: A famous philosopher once said hell is other people. Bold claim from a guy who never played mixed doubles.
slides: A famous philosopher once said 'hell is other people.' Bold claim from a guy who never played mixed doubles.
He also never had a group chat that planned a trip for two years and never once opened the calendar app.
Philosophy is great at describing the problem. It's historically bad at getting you off the sofa.
Here's a cheaper theory: hell is other people... you meet through a profile. Purgatory is other people you meet through a profile with three follow-up questions each.
Heaven is comparatively simple: an actual game, an actual time, whoever shows up.
We didn't solve existentialism. We just skipped the part where you overthink it. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio. 🎾
caption: Every philosopher has a theory about other people. None of them had to sit through 45 minutes of 'so... tell me about yourself' over lukewarm coffee. Our theory is cheaper: skip the interrogation, book the game, see who shows up. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Save this for the next time someone quotes philosophy at you to explain why they're single.
imageConcept: 6-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background, serif-quote styling on the first 2 cards (a subtle nod to a 'quote card' aesthetic — large opening quotation mark motif in neon-green, off-white serif-look bold text) shifting to KeepItUp's normal bold sans display type from card 3 onward as the tone turns from 'philosophy' to 'us.' Final card: KeepItUp wordmark centered, neon-green 'link in bio' arrow.

title: This Looks Like A Breakup Text Thread. It's Not.
trend: Fake-out / misdirection carousel — reads as one thing for several slides, plot-twists into something else entirely.
hook: We need to talk. It's not you, it's me. Actually — wait. Keep swiping. 👀
slides: "We need to talk."
"It's not you, it's me. Actually, that's not even true. It kind of is you."
"I've been getting these feelings for a while. Every night, actually. Usually around 11pm."
"I keep opening you just to close you two minutes later. I don't even know why anymore."
"I think... I think I need to delete you." — Plot twist: this isn't about a person. It's the group chat's read receipts to your dating app.
We're not a rebound. We're what happens after. Open worldwide — early access, link in bio. 🎾
caption: Every line of this is a breakup text. None of them are about a person — they're about the app. If you've had this exact internal monologue at 11pm this week, you already know what this post is going to say next. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment the emoji that matches your current relationship status with your dating app (be honest, not aspirational).
imageConcept: 6-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background styled like an intentionally generic messaging app (grey bubble, no real app logo/UI, no real names — a clearly mocked/abstracted chat bubble shape in muted grey-on-anthracite) for cards 1-4, building tension. Card 5: the bubble shape cracks/breaks apart with a neon-green highlight revealing the twist text. Card 6: full neon-green background, KeepItUp wordmark centered, off-white 'link in bio.'

title: The Algorithm vs. Real Life
trend: "The algorithm vs. real life" comparison format — a direct, funny side-by-side of what gets optimized vs. what actually matters.
hook: What the algorithm is optimizing for vs. what actually predicts if you'll like someone. They are not the same list.
slides: What the algorithm optimizes for vs. what actually predicts if you'll like someone. Spoiler: not the same list. 👇
The algorithm: response time under 3 minutes. Real life: whether they're fun to be stuck at 6-6 in a tiebreak with.
The algorithm: a curated 6-photo carousel. Real life: what their face does when they lose a point they really wanted.
The algorithm: streak count. Real life: whether they wait up or walk ahead.
The algorithm: a bio that says 'easygoing.' Real life: whether they actually are, under mild competitive pressure.
We can't out-optimize an algorithm. So we skipped it — an actual game tells you in 45 minutes what a profile can't tell you in 45 messages.
Open worldwide. Early access — link in bio. 🎾
caption: Dating apps got very good at optimizing for the things that are easy to measure — response time, streaks, a curated photo grid. None of those things tell you if you'll actually like being around someone. A real game does, in about 45 minutes. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Save this the next time a match's 'compatibility score' doesn't match how the conversation actually feels.
imageConcept: 7-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background, two-column comparison layout per inner card: left column labeled 'THE ALGORITHM' in muted grey-on-anthracite with a small flat phone-silhouette icon, right column labeled 'REAL LIFE' in bold neon-green with a running-figure/racket icon — the contrast in color/weight alone tells the joke. Final card: KeepItUp wordmark centered, neon-green 'link in bio' arrow.

title: Things Nobody Tells You About A 9am Sunday Padel Game With A Stranger
trend: "Things nobody tells you about ___" listicle-curiosity format.
hook: Things nobody tells you about showing up to a 9am Sunday game with someone you've never met. 👇
slides: Things nobody tells you about showing up to a 9am Sunday game with someone you've never met. 👇
1. The first 90 seconds are objectively awkward for both of you. This is normal. Nobody warns you it's normal.
2. By the third rally, nobody's performing anymore. You're just trying not to lose the point.
3. You will learn more about their personality from one bad line call than from a week of texting.
4. Somewhere around minute 20, you forget you were nervous. Nobody tells you exactly when it happens — it just does.
5. The walk to get water afterward is somehow the least awkward conversation you've had with a stranger in years.
6. You can't fake any of this over a chat thread. That's the whole point.
Open worldwide. Early access — link in bio to find yours. 🎾
caption: Nobody tells you the first 90 seconds of meeting a stranger for a game are awkward, on purpose, for everyone, every time. Nobody tells you it's usually gone by rally three either. Consider this your warning label. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Comment the number that hit closest to home from your own first-time-meeting-a-stranger experience.
imageConcept: 8-card carousel, 4:5, anthracite background, each numbered card uses a bold oversized neon-green numeral (1-6) top-left corner as the visual anchor, off-white body text beside/below it. Final card: KeepItUp wordmark centered, neon-green 'link in bio' arrow, small sunrise/morning-court icon nodding to '9am Sunday.'

title: POV: You're Reading Their Bio Out Loud To A Friend
trend: POV / plot-twist reel structure — misdirected setup, comedic reveal, on-brand payoff. Chaotic-cut pacing for retention.
hook: POV: you're reading your match's bio out loud to a friend and you can hear how it sounds the moment you say it.
script:
0:00-0:01 — On-screen text, no logo, straight into it: 'POV: reading your match's bio out loud to a friend.' Person holds phone, mouths reading silently (no real app UI shown — mocked generic profile card, no real names/photos).
0:01-0:04 — On-screen text quotes appear one at a time, fast cuts: '‘love to laugh’' → '‘down for anything’' → '‘just ask.’' Friend's face, unimpressed, deadpan, in-shot.
0:04-0:07 — Friend (no VO needed, just a mouthed line + on-screen text): 'so... you know nothing.' Comedic beat, freeze-frame.
0:07-0:10 — On-screen text: 'six paragraphs. zero information.' Rapid cut back to the phone, scrolling, scrolling, scrolling.
0:10-0:14 — Hard cut: same two friends, now mid-laugh at an actual small local game (padel/run/climb), daylight, no phones in shot. On-screen text: 'meanwhile, this took 45 minutes to tell you everything.'
0:14-0:18 — On-screen text only: 'you can't fake a backhand. you can absolutely fake a bio.'
0:18-0:22 — End card: KeepItUp mark on anthracite, on-screen text 'meet through movement — open worldwide, early access,' caption line: 'link in bio, save your friend the deadpan face.'
caption: There's a very specific silence that happens when you read a dating bio out loud to a friend and hear it for the first time. We just skip straight to the part with actual information: 45 minutes on a court tells you more than six paragraphs ever will. Open worldwide, early access — link in bio.
cta: Duet this with the most 'down for anything' bio line you've ever read out loud to someone.
imageConcept: Cover/thumbnail frame: anthracite background, bold neon-green on-screen text 'POV: reading their bio out loud' with a small deadpan-face emoji-style icon (illustrated, not a real photo), off-white subtext '...you can hear it too, right?', KeepItUp mark small bottom-right corner.
